text: This is a case of a seventy four-year-old male that presented to the emergency room with a GI bleed. The patient was on anticoagulation and after stopping his anticoagulation, endoscopy was performed and his gastroenterologist did identify a two point five centimeter mass just distal to the pylorus, therefore surgery was consulted. This is one of the first cases that I really used the TilePro technology robotically and I thought this would be a great opportunity to see if we can combine both the traditional three-dimensional robotic laparoscopic view with a gastroscope to try to really excise this tumor and keeping as much tissue as possible.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to identify and free a tattooed mass located anteriorly on the pylorus. Surrounding adhesions are carefully released, and a stay suture is placed in the mass for control. The robotic console displays both laparoscopic and endoscopic views, showing the pylorus and the mass clearly.

The surgeon uses a robotic SureForm stapler to excise the mass near the pylorus, ensuring a healthy margin of tissue is preserved. The gastroscope is positioned within the pylorus to assist in identifying the optimal area for stapling, while suture traction and counter-traction are applied to control the mass and avoid excessive tissue removal.

The surgeon uses the Robotic SureForm stapler to excise the mass with viable margins, employing two blue 60 SureForm staples. The resection is verified internally with a gastroscope and externally with a 3D robotic camera. Once freed, the mass is placed in an EndoCatch bag, and the specimen is opened on the back table to confirm clear margins.

Subsequently, pathology reviewed the mass and was found to be a neuroendocrine tumor with good margins. A drain was placed in the patient. And on postoperative day number one, the patient was started clear liquids. The drain was removed postoperative day number two. and the patient overall did very well and was discharged without any incident.

I really believe that this technology helped to really not only give me a view that I wouldn't normally have, seeing both the endoscopic view but the robotic view, and we were able to get just enough tissue to incorporate our mass and also have enough tissue and tissue left behind not to perform any stenosis on this patient, and I was very happy with the results. So I thought this was an interesting technique, and hopefully will be used in the future for different kinds of cases as well.
